title: Spotify API secrets should not be disclosed
description: In most cases, trust boundaries are violated when a secret is exposed in a source code repository or an uncontrolled deployment environment. Unintended people who don’t need to know the secret might get access to it. They might then be able to use it to gain unwanted access to associated services or resources.
recommendation: Review and fix according to the rule guidelines.

checks.message: Spotify API Client Secret detected in source code. Spotify uses OAuth2 authentication where the Client Secret is a 32-character hexadecimal string that serves as a confidential credential for authenticating API requests. This secret is used in conjunction with the Client ID to obtain access tokens for accessing Spotify's Web API, which provides access to music catalogs, user library data, playlists, playback control, user profiles, listening history, and recommendations. Exposing this Client Secret allows attackers to: impersonate your application and make unauthorized API requests consuming your rate limits, access user data if your app has been granted permissions including personal playlists, listening history, saved tracks, and user profiles, modify user data such as creating/deleting playlists, following/unfollowing artists, adding/removing tracks from libraries, control playback on user devices including playing, pausing, skipping tracks, and adjusting volume, exhaust your API rate limits (180 requests per minute) causing service disruption to legitimate users, access Spotify for Developers analytics revealing user engagement patterns and app usage statistics, potentially access premium features if your app subscription includes them, compromise user privacy by accessing detailed listening habits and music preferences, damage your application's reputation through abuse or spam activities, violate Spotify Developer Terms of Service resulting in app suspension or permanent ban, incur costs if using Spotify for Business APIs or premium tiers, and create fraudulent authorization flows to trick users into granting excessive permissions. For applications with large user bases, compromised Client Secrets can result in: mass unauthorized access to thousands or millions of user accounts, widespread playlist manipulation or spam, complete loss of API access if Spotify detects abuse, permanent blacklisting from Spotify Developer Platform, legal liability for data breaches under privacy regulations (GDPR, CCPA), loss of user trust and app uninstalls, emergency incident response and forensic investigation costs, and mandatory disclosure to affected users and regulators. IMMEDIATELY regenerate your Client Secret in Spotify Developer Dashboard at https://developer.spotify.com/dashboard (select your app → Settings → Show Client Secret → Regenerate), update all deployed instances with the new secret within the 30-day transition period before old secret expires, audit recent API activity in Spotify Dashboard checking for suspicious requests, unusual endpoints accessed, or unexpected geographic locations, review OAuth authorization logs for unauthorized token grants or excessive scope requests, check for unexpected changes to user data (playlists, follows, library) that might indicate abuse, verify rate limit usage patterns for anomalous spikes suggesting API key compromise, store Client Secrets securely using environment variables (SPOTIFY_CLIENT_SECRET) never hardcoded in source code, secret management systems (AWS Secrets Manager, HashiCorp Vault, Azure Key Vault, GCP Secret Manager), or encrypted configuration files (never commit to Git, Docker images, logs, or CI/CD pipelines), implement least privilege by requesting only necessary OAuth scopes (user-read-private, playlist-read-private, user-library-read) rather than excessive permissions, use PKCE (Proof Key for Code Exchange) flow for mobile and single-page applications to enhance security without exposing Client Secret, rotate Client Secrets regularly (every 90-180 days recommended as best practice), enable IP whitelisting if your application architecture uses static IP addresses for backend API calls, implement monitoring and alerting for: API rate limit approaching thresholds, requests from unexpected geographic locations or IP addresses, OAuth grants with suspicious scope combinations, user complaints about unauthorized playlist or library changes, and failed authentication attempts suggesting credential testing, use separate Client IDs and Secrets for development, staging, and production environments to isolate security blast radius, implement proper error handling to never log or expose Client Secret in error messages or debugging output, set up automated secret scanning in CI/CD pipelines to prevent accidental commits, maintain audit logs of all API key generation, rotation, and deletion events, use Spotify's Web API Console and Dashboard analytics to monitor application health and detect anomalies, implement rate limiting and request throttling in your application code to prevents abuse even with leaked credentials, and follow Spotify Developer Guidelines and Terms of Service to maintain compliance and good standing.
